title: The process of learning and socialisation
paperName: Paper 1: Socialisation, identity and methods of research
summary: 9699 — agencies of socialisation, nature/nurture, and how individuals learn norms and values.
learningObjectives: Define socialisation and distinguish primary from secondary socialisation.
Explain the role of key agencies: family, peers, media, religion, education.
Evaluate nature vs nurture in shaping behaviour.
Apply concepts to case examples in essays.

simpleExplanation.title: Learning to be social
simpleExplanation.summary: Socialisation is the lifelong process through which individuals internalise the norms, values, and roles of their society.
steps: Norms, values, roles, and culture transmitted.

flashcards.back: First learning of norms/values — mainly family in early childhood (Parsons).
Later learning via school, peers, media, workplace.
Family, education, peers, media, religion, workplace.
Suggests nurture essential — without social contact, 'human' behaviour does not develop normally.
Self develops through imagining how others see us.

sections.content: **Socialisation** (1.1) opens 9699 Paper 1. Name **agencies** and use **studies** (Mead, Parsons, Oakley) in evaluation. Always distinguish **primary** and **secondary**.

sections.question: Assess the view that the family is the most important agency of socialisation. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Intro:** Define socialisation — process of learning norms, values, roles.

**For family (primary):** Parsons — personality stabilisation; Oakley — gender role learning in early years; family has **intensive, emotional** contact when identity forms.

**Against / other agencies:** **Education** — hidden curriculum (Bowles & Gintis); **peers** — youth subcultures (Willis); **media** — global norms beyond family control; **class/ethnic** identity may conflict with family values.

**Evaluation:** Family is **foundational** but not sole — socialisation is **lifelong** and agencies **reinforce or contradict** each other. Strongest answers compare **primary vs secondary** and note **cultural variation** (e.g. extended family, same-sex parents).

**Conclusion:** Family remains **central** but **insufficient alone** to explain adult identity.
